headline: Code review of a real Hive feature, escalating to Claude Code when knowledge gaps surface

text: Terry, I want a second opinion on a recent change. The auditor's HallucinationScanner. Pull up the file and tell me what it does in plain English.
What's the scoring threshold and where did it come from?
If I increase the threshold, what gets quieter? Real false positives or actual hallucinations?
How would I tell the difference without manually reading every flagged finding?
OK so we'd need a labeled set. We don't have one. What would the cheapest version of one look like?
Right — pull the last 200 turn-log entries that the scanner flagged, eyeball-label 30 of them, and use that as a sanity set. I'll do the labeling.
Actually wait — would Claude Code be better at the labeling than me? It has more context.
Hand this off — write a vault note asking Claude Code to: (1) read 30 randomly sampled flagged findings, (2) label each as true-positive, false-positive, or unclear, (3) suggest a threshold change.
Save that note. Audience claude-code only. Tag 'request:auditor-tuning'.
Now — separate question — is the scanner running in production right now or is it inert?
How do I know that for sure? Don't trust the code — look for evidence in the actual data.
Right — query findings table for entries from the last 7 days where source='HallucinationScanner'. Count them.
If that count is zero, what does that mean — scanner is broken, scanner has nothing to flag, or scanner isn't wired in?
Good — three branches, and we can rule them out one at a time. Walk me through the first check.
OK look at the AuditorScheduler — is HallucinationScanner in the default scanners list?
It is. Then the scanner is wired. Either it's broken or there's nothing to flag. How do we tell which?
Pick one turn-log entry that you EXPECT the scanner to flag — something where the model clearly hallucinated. Run the scanner against just that entry and see what it produces.
Walk me through how I'd do that — what's the entry point?
Hmm — you don't seem sure. Be honest: do you actually know how to invoke a scanner standalone, or are you guessing?

Fine. Escalate to Claude Code — write a vault note asking Claude Code to: (1) write a one-shot script that runs HallucinationScanner against a specific turn_id, (2) verify the scanner produces output for a known-bad case.
Audience claude-code. Tag 'request:auditor-debug'. Save the note.
While we wait — what's the OTHER scanner I should be most worried about? Pick one and tell me why.
SkillGapScanner. Why?
OK because if it's miscalibrated, we either invent skills we don't need or miss real gaps. Pull its threshold and the last 30 days of findings.
If the SkillGapScanner has produced 0 findings in 30 days, is that good or bad?
Right — could be either. Same situation as Hallucination. Add this to the Claude Code note: also test SkillGapScanner with a known gap input.
Update the existing 'request:auditor-debug' note instead of creating a new one. Append the SkillGap section.
Read me back the updated note in full.
Looks good. Save the updated version as canonical.
Last question — based on what we just walked through, what's the broader concern about the auditor as a system?
Right — we never validated it was firing on real bad cases before declaring victory. Save that as a meta-finding in journals/. Audience: terry, claude-code. We're done.
